5 min read
Just now

--

Photo by Wyron A on UnsplashPrevious: 5. Reliability: how systems earn users’ trust by surviving failure(Part 3)
The Biggest Sale… That Never Happened
Imagine you’ve spent six months preparing for your company’s biggest event of the year.
It’s the Diwali Mega Sale.
The marketing team has worked day and night.
Thousands of products are discounted.
Influencers are promoting your brand.
Advertisements are running on YouTube, Instagram, and television.
Everyone is excited.
Finally…
The countdown reaches zero.
12:00 AM.
Within seconds, more than 300,000 customers rush to your website.
Some want smartphones.
Others want televisions.
Many are trying to grab limited-time offers before stock runs out.
Everything seems perfect…
Until customers begin seeing something they never expected.
“503 Service Unavailable.”
Some users refresh the page.
Nothing.
Others somehow reach the product page but can’t complete payment.
Many simply close the website and open Amazon or Flipkart instead.
By the time your engineers restore the service, thirty minutes have passed.
The business doesn’t just lose sales.
It loses trust.
Because customers don’t know whether your database failed.
They don’t know whether your servers crashed.
They don’t care whether AWS had an issue or someone deployed faulty code.
They only remember one thing.
“The website wasn’t available when I needed it.”
And that is today’s lesson.

Availability Isn’t About Perfect Software
In our previous article, we explored Reliability.
We learned that reliable systems continue functioning even when components fail.
Today we’re looking at another quality that often gets confused with reliability.
Availability.
At first, they sound almost identical.
But they answer different questions.
Reliability asks,
“Can my system continue working correctly over time?”
Availability asks,
“Can users access my system right now?”
Imagine a hospital.
It has the best doctors.
Modern equipment.
Experienced nurses.
Advanced operation theaters.
Everything is world-class.
But the hospital doors are locked.
Would anyone call that hospital useful?
Of course not.
No matter how excellent the service inside is, patients cannot benefit if they can’t get in.
Software works exactly the same way.
Your application might have incredible features.
Lightning-fast performance.
Outstanding security.
But if users can’t open it…
None of those qualities matter.
Availability is simply the ability of a system to remain accessible whenever users need it.

Every Minute Offline Has a Price
When I first started learning System Design, I assumed downtime was mostly an engineering inconvenience.
Then I started reading incident reports from large companies.
That’s when I realized…
Downtime is measured in money.
Imagine an e-commerce company generating ₹15 crore in sales every day.
That means approximately:
Time -> Estimated Revenue
Per Day -> ₹15 Crore
Per Hour -> ₹62.5 Lakh
Per Minute-> ₹1.04 Lakh
Now imagine the application remains unavailable for 45 minutes during a festive sale.
The direct revenue loss alone is nearly ₹47 lakh.
But that’s only the visible damage.
The hidden cost is often much larger.
Customers abandon their carts.
Support centers become overwhelmed.
Marketing campaigns continue spending money while the website is offline.
Social media begins filling with complaints.
Some customers never return.
Suddenly, what looked like “just a server issue” becomes a business crisis.
This is why CTOs and CEOs discuss availability in the same meeting.
Because availability isn’t just an engineering metric.
It’s a business metric.

The Internet Never Closes
Think about your favorite applications.
Netflix.
WhatsApp.
Google.
Spotify.
Amazon.
Have you ever wondered when these companies perform maintenance?
Years ago, many businesses displayed messages like:
“Website Under Maintenance. Please Visit Tomorrow.”
Today, that message has almost disappeared.
Why?
Because modern software serves the entire world.
When India sleeps…
Europe is working.
When Europe sleeps…
America is shopping.
Someone is always online.
There is no perfect maintenance window anymore.
Today’s users expect software to work:

• Morning

• Afternoon

• Midnight

• Weekends

• Holidays

• Festivals
Twenty-four hours a day.
Seven days a week.
Three hundred sixty-five days a year.
Meeting that expectation is one of the biggest engineering challenges in modern software.

Measuring Something We Cannot See
Suppose someone proudly tells you,
“Our application has excellent availability.”
That sounds impressive.
But what does “excellent” actually mean?
Engineering doesn’t work with adjectives.
It works with numbers.
Availability is measured using one of the simplest formulas in System Design.
Availability = Total Uptime
──────────────────── × 100
Total TimeSuppose your application runs continuously for one month.
A month contains:
30 Days = 43,200 Minutes
Now imagine your application experiences an outage lasting 43 minutes.
Your uptime becomes:
43,157 minutes
Availability becomes:
43,157
──────── × 100
43,200
≈ 99.90%That single outage reduced your availability to 99.9%.
At first glance…
99.9% sounds almost perfect.
But let’s see what those percentages actually mean.

The Tiny Decimal That Costs Millions
Engineers often talk about something called “The Five Nines.”
The more nines after the decimal point…
The more difficult — and expensive — it becomes to keep the system online.
Here’s what those numbers really mean.
Availability -> Maximum Downtime
Per Year -> 99%
3 Days 15 Hours -> 99.9%
8 Hours 46 Minutes -> 99.95%
4 Hours 23 Minutes -> 99.99%
52 Minutes -> 99.999%
(Five Nines) 5 Minutes 15 Seconds
Take a moment to look at that table.
Moving from 99% to 99.999% reduces downtime from nearly four days every year…
To just five minutes.
That tiny decimal point represents:

• Extra servers

• Multiple databases

• Redundant networks

• Automatic failover

• Disaster recovery

• Continuous monitoring

• Highly skilled engineering teams
Those extra “9s” aren’t free.
They’re earned through architecture.

Why Google Search Rarely Goes Offline
Think about the last thousand times you searched something on Google.
Can you remember seeing Google’s homepage unavailable?
Most people can’t.
That’s not because Google’s servers never fail.
In fact…
Servers fail every single day.
Hard disks die.
Memory modules fail.
Network switches malfunction.
Entire data centers occasionally experience problems.
Google expects all of that.
Instead of preventing every failure…
It designs systems that continue operating despite failures.
If one server becomes unavailable…
Traffic moves to another.
If one rack fails…
Another rack takes over.
If an entire region experiences problems…
Requests are redirected elsewhere.
Failures happen constantly.
Users rarely notice.
That’s the real meaning of High Availability.
Not avoiding failures…
But hiding them from customers.

High Availability Isn’t One Technology
One of the biggest misconceptions among beginners is believing there’s a product called “High Availability.”
There isn’t.
High Availability is an architectural approach.
It’s achieved by combining multiple techniques together.
Think of it like building a modern hospital.
Backup electricity alone isn’t enough.
You also need:

• Backup oxygen

• Backup internet

• Backup ambulances

• Backup medical staff

• Emergency procedures

• Continuous monitoring
Software follows exactly the same philosophy.
Every important component needs a backup.
Every failure needs a recovery plan.
Every outage should affect as few users as possible.
In the next section, we’ll design a production-ready High Availability architecture and see how companies like AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud make this possible.
